author: @Xanderlxe
text:
Video ideas
1. Would a hollow ball go further than a solid ball?
2. What would happen if two black holes collided?
3. The correct way to prune a bonsai tree.
4. How do cameras work?
5. How do eyes work?
6. Why does the color white reflect light and keep you cooler, and why does black absorb light and make you hotter?
7. Why do you get sunburns?
8. How are transparent things see-through?
9. Is water wet?
10. Why is water transparent?
11. Why is the sky blue?
12. Why is the ocean blue?
13. If the sky is blue why is the sun yellow?
14. Why is driftwood often a shiny silver color?
15. How are LED’s different colors of light?
16. How do LED’s work?
17. If there is a trampoline as big as the earth and all 8 billion people on earth double bounced one person would that person exit the atmosphere?
18. How do batteries work?
19. How do solar panels work?
20. Is space cold or warm?
21. If heat rises how come the higher up you are the colder it is?
22. If the ENTIRE bottom of the ocean was electricity-proof how much electricity would it take in order to electrify the ENTIRE ocean.
23. If the earth was next to a different sun with a different light spectrum, would the colors we see daily be different? Would the leaves on trees be orange?
24. What would happen if every single tree variant was grafted into a single tree? how big would it be? How small of a fragment of every tree would it have to be in order to fit into a single tree?
25. How much energy would be required in order to boil the salt out of the entire ocean.
26. Why are the things you hear louder after you yawn?
27. What would happen if you peed off of a helicopter?
video_title: This 3D Printer Uses A Mosquito 😮

author: @TheXangelus
text: Honestly i’d love something like this to use as a sketchbook. It would be so nice to have a sketchbook that I can freely remove or even reorder the pages of it to my linking. You could even have multiple kinds of paper from watercolor to drawing paper for example, able to have allot more choice in which medium i’d like to work. Also there is nothing better than a good sketch book that lies completely flat. It’s so annoying when the pages keep flipping because it doesn’t want to lie flat. Another thing is i’m liking is the fact that normal sketchbook books are pretty beat up when I’ve completely filled it. This seems allot less likely to completely fall apart due to using it allot like normal sketchbooks tend to do. Most art shops sell all kinds of paper that size so i’m hoping he releases this soon so I can order one, or if it’s possible to print it to sell the file so I can print it myself. Definitely keeping an eye out, because for me this has allot of potential for artists.

author: @gkrpgfan
text:
Okay so this is what happened for all those interested:

So Windows 11 requires the Trusted Platform Module to run. Most likely, it was enabled on the BIOS level then Windows 10 was upgraded to Windows 11.

However, the user must have unplugged the PC and left it unplugged for a couple days and, with the CMOS battery dead, those settings disappeared. Therefore, the system would no longer boot as TPM was now disabled again.

author: @SalemTechsperts
text:
HOW I DID THIS:
Plug a FAT32-formatted USB drive into the PS3.
Go to Saved Data Utility (PS3), locate your Red Dead Redemption save, press Triangle, and select Copy to the USB.

Connect the USB to your PC.
Download and open Bruteforce Save Data.
Point the application to your PS3 save folder on the USB (typically starting with
BLUS or BLES).
Click Decrypt PFD to remove the encryption.

Download the Red Dead Redemption Save
Editor by XB36Hazard.
Open your decrypted PS3 save file within the editor.
Change the target platform to PC (if the
PC option is missing in your specific build of the editor, select PS4/Nintendo Switch, as the modern ports utilize the exact same file structure).
Save the newly converted file.
• Load the Save on PC
Open your PC save directory located at:
Documents|Rockstar Games\Red Dead
Redemption\Profiles\[Your Profile ID|\
Move your converted save file into this folder.
Launch the game and load the file.

My biggest issue was BruteForce not decrypting properly. I had to then download RPCS3 and a Red Dead Redemption iso, play it until it saved the file, download Apollo save tool homebrew, swap the RPCS3 save file with the one from my PS3, then decrypt it inside Apollo.

I DONT KNOW IF THERE ARE OTHER GAMES THAT CAN DO THIS
Don’t ask me for help cause I just followed directions and don’t know anything.

author: @lottalotto691
text:
1. Satellites track your location, plus other tools in the phone to estimate your location while driving.
2. Traffic data is pooled mostly from other people using the same gps app or apps using the same data broker. 
3. Trends are developed over time to best calculate routes, traffic, and timing.
4. Map data is mostly open source meaning there are many contributors from all around the world to fill in street names, businesses, events, etc.
5. Routes are calculated with weighted pathing algorithms like A* (a-star), and fine tuned based on trends, like popular routes and traffic.

author: @Sec7ionSevenRIGS
text:
Fantastic work by the entire GN team.

Honestly, when I saw the runtime, I thought there was no chance I'd watch all 3+ hours. Yet somehow I ended up watching every minute. That's a testament to both the research and the storytelling.

My biggest takeaway is that personal computing itself isn't collapsing — but DIY enthusiast computing may be losing its place as a mainstream consumer activity.

What concerns me most isn't just rising prices or shrinking product selection. It's the gradual erosion of ownership, control, repairability, and freedom of choice as the industry shifts toward AI, cloud services, subscriptions, and enterprise-scale customers.

In a strange way, DIY PCs may become the vinyl records of computing: more niche, more expensive, and pursued primarily by people who genuinely love the craft.

Thought-provoking, exceptionally researched, and well worth the time.
video_title: COLLAPSE of Personal Computing | Investigation Into the Destruction of Ownership

author: @ImmensityYT
text:
*The smartest part of DuoBell is that it still sounds exactly like a normal bike bell!*

That’s because the innovation was never about making a louder or futuristic sound.

Nearly 50% of headphones sold today include active noise cancellation. 

Researchers also reported a 24% rise in cyclist-pedestrian collisions linked to reduced environmental awareness from ANC headphone use.

So Škoda partnered with researchers at the University of Salford to study how modern noise-cancelling systems react to traditional bike bells.

They discovered a small “safety gap” between 750–780 Hz that many ANC systems struggle to fully block. 

DuoBell was engineered around that frequency, combined with an irregular striking pattern designed to make the sound harder for noise-cancelling algorithms to suppress in real time.

During testing, pedestrians wearing ANC headphones reportedly noticed the bell up to 22 meters earlier and gained around 5 extra seconds to react.

A simple mechanical bike bell quietly exposing the limits of modern technology is what makes this campaign so brilliant.
____

author: @Shuksanaudio
text:
Thanks so much for watching! This restoration cost the client about $1300. The meter inserts were designed in Adobe illustrator and I went through 7 print shops to find one that could do this. It's 6 layers of ink to block out light. It took me about 3 and a half months from design to completed prints.

**AC DC used to be the song that this video ended on but youtube decided to block the video from being viewed after 1.5million views unless I removed the song so that's why it ends all funky

author: @sf-jim8885
text: I've seen the reverse happen on a professional monitor, where the screen was blank, but if I bounced a flashlight off the screen, I could see a very dim picture, which meant that all the video circuits were working OK but the CFL backlight was out. With some simple troubleshooting I figured out that the CFL driver board was bad. These driver boards are fairly standard among many brands of TV's & monitors. I found the part number stenciled on the circuit board, and ordered an identical one online, and that fixed the problem. So, for $45 I fixed a professional monitor that it would have taken almost $4k to replace. My boss was very happy.

author: @DSedesse
text: I’ve worked in the electronic section at Costco for years. None of the products are B grade. In fact most of them even including small appliances, like vacuums, for example, typically have additional features that you wouldn’t get on that product somewhere else. author: @BrentWingfield
text: Any Costco price that ends with .97 indicates it is a clearance item.  Typically, those clearance items have a significant price reduction.  However, items are put on clearance by the individual store managers, meaning that one store might have the item on clearance, but other Costcos might have the exact same item at the regular Costco retail price.

author: @frollard
text:
The reason they do costco exclusive part numbers is so that nobody can price match them.

Everything sold at costco is on consignment - (also why they take returns for absurdly long)  - the manufacturer bids to get access to the sales floor of costco and they have to be bargain basement prices to survive on the floor. If it doesn't sell they just go to auction, the manufacturer doesn't get it back.  (by default, without a huge 'fee').

The manufacturers can't afford to have everyone selling at wholesale prices so they don't allow price matching.

author: @CaryGordon3k
text: I used to work for an electronics manufacturer that sold their products in Costco as well. At the time the only difference between the model sold at Costco and the standard model was the model number. The thing is that the warranty was doubled when sold through Costco, which was immediately adjusted for in our ticketing system when repair tickets were opened. When items couldn't be repaired Costco immediately swapped out for a replacement unit and our RMA was done through the Costco, super convenient.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @AmosAAnderson
text:
No, the products at Costco are not B-Line rates. Often the difference is that Costco can sell x number of products and so can do it cheaper, but to sell the same products at that much lower than MSRP is not fair to other retailers. They will sometimes change what comes with it, change the warranty, etc. but very often the model number change is just so they can say they're not selling it under MSRP because it's not the same as the product everyone else is selling. This is not a new practice and has been going on between Costco and Sam's Club for many years. I used to do customer service/tech support for several large television, camera, etc. companies and that was pretty much what we were told. I've never seen it be different and I buy Costco all the time.

Buying something at Costco is almost always and upgrade, not a downgrade.

author: @Durakken
text:
Costco prices are based on a different metric than the average store. Basically. There is this accounting that most stores do of their inventory where they say they can go through their inventory 3 times a year for example and then they negotiate with manufacturers based on that number. Most stores metrics say they'll only go through a few times, if I remember right 3-6. Costco pushes that they will go through their inventory something like 20-30 times a year. To do this requires that they price lower and work with manufactures, which they can because manufactures can then move more of their product.

This is not the case with Walmart though. They just have so much domination over the market that they demand lower prices when you see them.

author: @RapidNack
text:
As someone who works in the Consumer Packaged Goods industry and sells to Costco, I can tell you first-hand that they genuinely force companies to accept lower margins, while handing a majority of your own (the company's) margin to Costco. This is still seen as a good deal as Costco moves a lot of volume and it ends up still being profitable for the company. 

To top this off, sometimes companies will actually have very slightly different products that vary for Costco/club channels. A good example of this is the Sony carrying case. Sometimes though, the product itself will be worse.

Overall, Costco is always going to be a great deal for the customer, still a good deal for the company/seller, and even better for Costco

author: @LogicalNiko
text: The biggest problem with early ammonia adsorption refrigeration is that they have to be constructed very well with heavy lines.  This is because high pressure heated ammonia is extremely flammable.  In fact early in industrial refrigeration explosions were a pretty big risk.  The other factor is the anhydrous ammonia if leaked would react with water to form ammonium hydroxide and could self ignite as well as cause suffocation in a closed room.

author: @joezegers
text:
There's a "big seven" when it comes to browsers right now.
Edge: If you're too lazy to install a browser on Windows.
Chrome: If you want a browser that is reliable.
Firefox: If you want a browser that is more secure than Chrome. It's also the Linux equivalent of Edge.
Safari: The MacOS equivalent of Edge.
Brave: If you want Chrome with more privacy.
Opera: If you think basic browsers are too mainstream.
Vivaldi: If you think Opera is *_still_* too mainsteam for you.

author: @Ten_Mil_Will
text:
Another way to view this is:  Plumbing, Electrical, Structural.

Arteries (always preferable to veins) fall under plumbing, nerve bundles are electrical, and connective tissue like tendons are structural. None of em matter, tho, if you don't have a good understanding of WHERE they are and what is required (force wise) to damage each.

author: @Andrew-cm5tc
text: He probably doesn't know that lots of nerves, arteries, and veins usually travel together. Your axillary nerves (median, ulnar, and radial nerves) in your armpit are touching the axillary artery. The femoral nerve is right next to the femoral artery. I've also seen someone who got shot in the femoral artery and without the tourniquet, it bled like a geyser. It did not "retract" and stop bleeding.

author: @dillenbeck53531
text:
Good initial purchase cost and performance review.  My questions: what is the comparative cost after 1 screen and 1 keyboard repair, including turn around time for the repair? Costs to use with external monitor, keyboard, mouse, and external drive storage?  What if you discover you need more ram or better cpu? What if you realize taking calculus and chemistry notes are better input with a stylus, even if it is slightly laggy? (Add the cost of an iPad with stylus to see which option is cheaper.) 

Basically Apple is buying a disposable razor and Framework is buying a safety razor that takes standard razor blades. Sure the first time cost is way lower -but constant replacement costs at a low price will make the higher investment item lifetime costs far cheaper.

Of course, there is the whole software compatibility issue for students also. If you're taking engineering classes with CAD software that only runs on windows, then neither of these will do.
video_title: The Framework 12 is dead. Apple killed it.

I really want to like the Framework Laptops alot, but they are SO much more expensive.  It's just hard to justify the 50% price premium over, say, an HP or Thinkpad Laptop and that's a real shame. 
I'll be in the market in the near future for a replacement for my 10yr old HP Envy laptop, probably around Black Friday timeframe this year, and I'd really like the replacement to be a Framework 13 Laptop, but cost will probably dictate that I get another HP or a Thinkpad.  Oh well...
Good video Jeff!  Thanks for this!!

author: @dawson6767
text:
I dont want to be the bearer of bad news but this actually doesnt hurt your battery life, ion-lithium batterys have a BMS that stop the flow of power to the battery at 100% the green light staying on doesnt use the battery just the power from the plug. If ion lithium batterys took charge after 100% they would overcharge and not be pretty. Hope this helps

Ps: to keep ion lithium batterys life longer charge when you reach 50% this is the recommened, you can play until its low anyway. But they recommened 20-50% to charge it to keep battery life the longest

They value what you do on the computer and not the computer itself because they can instantly get another one and have an expert prepare it for them with virtually no effort. Having the computer at all is invisible to them.
video_title: guess we're not buying computers anymore...

author: @migidid
text:
1st-person camera view gives a massive visibility advantage over 3rd-person chase cams through corners and crests.
In 3rd person, the camera pivots behind the car, meaning the vehicle's own body literally blocks your view of the apex or the downward slope of a hill.
Moving the camera forward completely eliminates that blind spot. Because your viewpoint is right over the front axle, the camera shifts instantly the moment you steer, rather than waiting for the rear of the car to swing around. It gives you millimeter-precision for clipping curbs and lets you see through blind corners way earlier, even if you lose a bit of situational awareness of cars right next to your rear quarter panel.

text:
0:44 That's an LPN. It means it's been bought from Amazon, returned, and processed back into the system, and then resold on Amazon. Anything that gets returned and processed through an Amazon Relo site will have an LPN. Whether it gets resold as new, WHD (warehouse deals), unsellable (return to vendor), liquidation (wrapped batch on pallet usual for super cheap or at auction), donation, or destroy/recycle.

I work at a relo site.

text: LPN (License Plate Number) is Amazon's way of tracking customer returned units. It can either be new or unsellable. author: @Kraaketaer
text:
As a non-apple user who has been quite critical of them throughout the years, I really like a lot of what they're doing with their more budget minded options these days. Unfortunately, their products never seem to fit the bill for me, as I tend to oscillate between cheap and high(ish)-end in a way that doesn't really align with what Apple offers. The MB Neo might change that though - it's the only Apple product that I have genuinely considered buying (with the halfway exception of the M4 Mac Mini), with the main reason for me not having done so being the display quality (I have a 14" OLED laptop, and while the Neo would be much smaller, lighter, and have vastly better battery life, I would *really* miss that display). 

But other than that? 
- I'm not a tablet user, but if I was to get one I'd want a good pen and display - but the Air starts at far too high a price for 128GB of storage, the Pro is just too expensive for an occasional use product (even if that display looks amazing), and the iPad just won't cut it.
- for earbuds, my CMF buds 2 deliver much better sound quality than the AirPods for half the price, and have (good!) ANC to boot, and if I want good audio quality I'll use my wired Sennheisers
- I find the health/fitness aspects of the Watch compelling, but beyond that and notifications i don't actually want a "smart" watch, and my basic, entry level Garmin does a lot of that, has a sunlight readable display, and week-long battery life for half the price. Charging my watch every day would drive me crazy.
- as for the iPhone ... Again I feel like the display is the major drawback. The accessory ecosystem is amazing, and the phones are by all accounts very good, but I'm not paying for a Pro, and giving up 120Hz would be very rough. I'm not all that happy with my Pixel 8 (honestly its battery life is disappointing, especially with performance being mediocre for its generation), but I'd still take it over an iPhone from the same generation (and changing phones before you have to is, at this point, just dumb).

And, crucially: once I start looking for Apple products that might to some level meet my wants and needs, the prices shoot up massively. An iPad air with acceptable storage adds a huge chunk to the price. Even the Pencil is wildly expensive. The watches just don't have acceptable battery life, and Macs other than the Neo and M4 Mini have the same issue of astronomical price increases once you add a few minor upgrades.

Seems to me like the value proposition really *only* exists at the very low end and with the base models - which is great if that's all you need, but for those of us who have worked in sales, we know what being set up for a high margin upsell looks like.

author: @Coffe1Coffe
text:
Before people come here arguing about the prices, this is just my opinion on it. I don’t think Craighill’s products are overpriced for the level of design they offer. There are brands like Gucci selling items at much higher prices that, in my opinion, don’t even come close to the thought and craftsmanship this company puts into its work.

Like any luxury or premium brand, these products obviously aren’t everyday purchases for everyone, and that’s understandable. But personally, I think they’re beautifully made, and the attention to detail is exceptional.

I do understand the criticism about “creating solutions for problems that don’t exist,” but I think the whole point of this company, and their videos, is to showcase the design process and the amount of thought behind every object they create.

As Steve Jobs once said: “Design is not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.”
video_title: The ideal measuring device for us didn't exist, so we designed a new one.

author: @Dimmers
text: I have developed my company's AI Phone Agent, and it makes sense that it can't/won't answer questions about shopping at Target or 1+1. It's sticking to its focus and not stepping outside that may be against brand or business guidelines. I personally think it handled okay-ish. That said, for my company's AI phone agent, we added rephrasing contextual awareness back to the user. For example, in one instance, we had someone testing the system to ask how to bake a cake. Our agent replied along the lines of "While I can't help you bake a cake I can help you with X". This way, the user feels understood while staying within scope and guiding the user back to its core focus.

text: One of the big reasons SIs are able to come in so low in cost right now is that they are buying in bulk (economy of scale) combined with having an average 3 month or more lag between when they buy the parts on contract and when they get the final product to market which means the price increases on parts hit them at a delay. If/when the prices ever come back down that will become a bane for them as it will take months for them to bring their own prices down to match or they will have to eat that extra cost.

author: @BruceLeedar
text: I don't even own an OLED (aside from my phone), but I still feel that for serious TV viewing the luminance detail of per-pixel lighting is critical for any high end display experience. While blooming artifacts can be skillfully reduced, there is no way for conventional array dimming LCD displays to replicate the crisp high contrast image detail that an OLED can. You're not getting a real 4K presentation when dimming zones are used with a high contrast image, it's more like an interpolation of 4K with whatever the dimming zone resolution is (to say nothing of dimming lag that afflicts most TVs which causes dynamic inaccuracies in high motion scenes).

author: @InfansWatch
text: This one-bowl recipe yields rich, fudgy brownies with a perfectly crackly top.IngredientsUnsalted butter: 1 cup (226g), melted and slightly cooledGranulated white sugar: 2 cups (400g)Unsweetened cocoa powder: ¾ cup (75g)Large eggs: 3, at room temperatureVanilla extract: 1 tablespoonAll-purpose flour: 1 cup (120g)Salt: 1 teaspoonSemisweet chocolate chips: 1 ½ cups (270g)Step-by-Step InstructionsPrep the oven and pan: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C). Lightly grease an 8×8-inch metal baking pan and line it with parchment paper for easy removal.Whisk the base: In a large bowl, combine the melted butter, granulated sugar, and cocoa powder. Whisk vigorously for 30 seconds. This dissolves some of the sugar, which is the secret to getting that shiny, crackly top.Add wet ingredients: Whisk in the eggs, vanilla extract, and salt until the mixture is smooth and fully incorporated.Fold in dry ingredients: Add the flour and chocolate chips to the bowl. Switch to a rubber spatula and gently fold the mixture together just until the dry flour disappears. Do not overmix, or the brownies will become tough and cakey.Bake: Spread the thick batter evenly into your prepared pan. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes.Test for doneness: Insert a toothpick into the center. For ultra-fudgy brownies, it should come out with a few moist crumbs or a slight smear of batter, not completely clean.Cool and slice: Let the brownies cool completely in the pan on a wire rack before lifting them out and slicing into squares. They will firm up as they cool.

author: @macca_usa
text:
♥ I sold my entire audio setup to buy a plane ticket to America and marry my dream girl. 
Hey Randy! Back in Australia, I had a proper system, the kind that rattled the walls with Cold Chisel, Midnight Oil, and The Angels at volumes that would make the neighbours file a formal complaint. It was glorious.
But love makes you do crazy things. So I recently packed a bag, left the gear behind, and flew halfway around the world to put a ring on the most incredible woman I've ever met.
Now I'm here in the US, married to my dream girl and completely, devastatingly speaker-less.
No Barnesy. No Diesel. No "am I ever gonna see your face again?" echoing through the living room at 9pm on a Friday. Just silence, and a Spotify queue full of bangers with nowhere to go.
The Klipsch The 7 II's would be the perfect reunion, streaming straight from my phone, no need to rebuild a whole component setup from scratch. Just pure, room-filling sound, the way music was meant to be heard.
And I promise, just like I vowed to my wife, I will have and to hold these speakers, and fully listen to them, until death do us part. 🤘🖤
She calls it our love story... I call it Phase 1 of rebuilding the audio setup. 🤣

author: @BXMalfroy
text:
My setup is a labor of love — every piece of gear carefully chosen, every cable run with intention. But there's one gap I haven't been able to fill: a powered speaker that can actually breathe in a room without me babying a receiver and a pile of separates. Klipsch The Fives II would've been the answer — but The Sevens II? That's the answer and then some.
Here's what I'd do: I'd put them in my main listening space, hook them up to my turntable and my DAC, and just... stop chasing. No more "maybe if I swap the amp." No more forum rabbit holes at 2am. Just music, the way it's supposed to sound — dynamic, alive, and filling the room the way Klipsch has always done better than anyone.
I've watched your channel because you cut through the noise and actually tell the truth about gear. Winning these would mean I finally get to experience the speaker you clearly believe in — and trust me, I'd report back. You'd hear from me. 🙏

I remember reading about this aircraft. It was one of those ideas which came up in the 50s and 60s which I would read about in the Eagle comic. Another one they wrote about was the aircraft which could land on water and then submerge like a submarine. It looked a lot like the F111. If the Fairey Rotodyne had gone into production the next stage was also to use the stowed rotor concept. As a gyrodyne which used hot gases to spin the rotor it was less complex than a conventional helicopter rotor so it would have been easier to stow the blades.

author: @nolsmtm
text: Retreating blade stall does not make the helicopter want to flip over but rather just slows it down again. The rolling moment you might expect from one side of the rotor disk losing lift actually acts at 90 degrees due to gyroscopic precession, resulting in a pitch up moment, slowing the craft down, it is self regulating.

author: @MarkEichin
text: About a decade ago, some researchers working on getting quadrotor drones to fail more sanely (controlled landings with multiple engine failures) did a bunch of work on the "monorotor" limit case - one motor, one asymmetric blade.  Since it was a drone, it was ok that it vibrated madly - "angry that it existed" really captures it pretty well.   (They did figure out how to land it in a controlled manner... it couldn't take off, though, you had to throw it into the air and let it recover from that to "fly".)  Fascinating to see that there was an almost-built "grandparent" of the concept...

author: @richardheeth6218
text: Many YT videos on the evolution of next generation rotorcraft. But everyone seems to forget the one developed by LTV the manufacturer of the F-8, A-7 and the WW II Corsair. They developed the XC-142 a 4 engine tilt-wing in the early 60's.  It could carry 30+ soldiers or 4 tons of cargo, top speed of 375 kt, cruise speed of 250 kt, combat range of up to 450 mi, ceiling of 25,000 ft. Only one remaining. I remember it sitting on the NW corner of the LTV plant for years. It is now on display at Wright-Pat.

author: @danpatterson8009
text: I see two fundamental problems. 1) The system will only be in dynamic balance at one rotor rpm (though that might not be a serious issue depending on how the "helicopter envelope" is defined), and 2) the lift from the single blade will try to bend the rotor shaft sideways and will be hard on whatever bearings are trying to hold the shaft in place. In a multi-blade setup the bending forces from opposing blades cancel each other out.
video_title: The helicopter with... one blade

author: @hWat-Ever
text:
"push it too hard and the whole aircraft wants to flip over"

No. The retreating blade stalls while the advancing blade produces more lift. And because of gyroscopic procession that lifts the nose and dips the tail.

So the helicopter can't accelerate further. It doesn't "flip over"

author: @Arcticstar0
text:
My opinion on stick drift is that there are now technologies that avoid it and are not exorbitantly expensive. It is a choice to choose potentiometer based joy sticks. And it’s a choice for these companies to not handle recalibration themselves.
That’s in addition to access to information and complaint amplification.

author: @Anonymoosus
text: Former employee here: Sales Specialists check for serial number and IMEI on the trade-in iPhones using a work iPhone, not iPad, during the point of sale. They then use a tool that assesses the iPhone’s physical condition and the internal functionality via software. If everything passes, the customer gets full value trade in applied during their purchase. If there are failures detected, the trade-in value will significantly be reduced to a fraction or $0 (which will then recommend a recycle). Trade-in’s can also be done post purchase, which not many people know about.
video_title: Inside Apple's Secretive Trade-In Program

author: @donoflee
text:
Greg,

You’ve overlooked a crucial engineering aspect that Apple incorporated into this approach, which makes it successful. They equip iPhones (and MacBooks, iPads, and Apple Watches) with powerful processors, ensure their durability and reliability, and provide feature upgrades and software support for an extended period. Consequently, a two- to three-year-old middle-tier iPhone retains significant value because it’s actually competitive with many other new smartphones.

In contrast, the approach favored by many manufacturers involves creating a product primarily made of plastic, featuring a bare minimum processor, and setting the lowest price point. Subsequently, they abandon the product a year later. I suspect, but haven’t calculated it, that buying a three-year-old middle-tier iPhone and keeping it for three years is currently one of the best deals on the planet.

author: @tdkz72
text: The short answer is there are no good options except for a fiber run to your home. For travelers Starlink is as good as it gets. Cellular is actually okay in most cases unless you are doing something bandwidth demanding like zoom calls or day trading stocks. You would be foolish to try those on any wireless solution.  I finally had fiber run to my neighborhood and made the switch about 3 years ago and have never looked back. I think I have been down once and only because a wasp ate into the fiber run inside the dmarc outside my house. The repair guy knew exactly what it was and sealed the box after repairing my fiber. It has worked perfectly ever since! I used to have cableTV + Internet and it would go out all of the time. It was very expensive and frustrating. I was glad to get rid of it!

author: @AdvanceForward-mn8eu
text:
This could be an option for your home, but if you're traveling the national forest and deserts across the US like me, cell is not a reasonable option compared to Starlink, unless you want to burn a lot of money and power, and only have coverage in some locations. No matter what sort of antenna you have, with cell Internet, you still have to reach a cell tower. With Starlink, all I need is a sky. 

I have a four-wheel drive, not an RV, so this allows me to go to the most beautiful and private places without costing me a penny in campground fees. No matter where I've been, and I have been all over the US: thick forest, deep valleys, over a hundred miles from people, etc, with Starlink, I always have Internet. It also uses very little power compared to these expensive cell antennas, and costs a lot less. I can't even fathom why a mobile outdoor person would choose cell over Starlink. I don't even have a cell plan, because I use Starlink to make phone calls.
video_title: The Alternative to Starlink You Never Knew Existed

author: @chrisball3778
text: I knew the twist because I visited a fort in Malta where they have one of Armstrong's inventions- the 100-ton gun. It's the biggest muzzle-loading cannon ever created and it's one of the most insanely steampunk things I've ever seen. It could fire a shell that weighed as much as a small car 6 kilometres. It was loaded with hydraulics and it's trajectories were calculated with slide rules. In the 1870's Armstrong offered it to the Royal Navy, but they turned it down. So he sold some to the Italian Navy. Then he went back to the British and pointed out that the Italians had a bigger gun than them now. So they had to buy some as well. There are two of them left- the one in Malta that I saw, and another one in Gibraltar.

author: @greenwizard9878
text:
Finally something I can comment on. I have Xreal One. They work perfectly fine for gaming. I played Outer Wilds, it was amazing experience. I tried to do some coding, it was less than ideal. RN they're mostly used to watch some movies via iPhone.
IPD adjustable on the software for the cost of resolution. It has support for 3D videos and virtual widescreen. It can "fix" the screen in one place, and you could look away from it if you need to do so.  Sound quality is good, but sound bleed is crazy. Top of the glasses become warm after 10 minutes or so, it might be uncomfortable. they are quite front heavy and could slip from your nose if you tilt you head low enough. nose piece is flexible and you movement may result in loss of focus until you physically move glasses back in place.

All in all, I don't use them very often. But they are neat addition then I have to travel or just want to lay in bed watching some film. Definitely a niche product

author: @stevewarshauer1356
text: I have been using this Fitbit Air since Saturday and weight training does not show any heart rate at all.  When discussing with the AI Coach, it said that this device can not detect heart rate when doing this type of activity at all.  I even tried it using the manual start and tried it under weight lifting and strength training and neither recorded any heart rate at all.  For indoor cycling which I do 5 days per week for 10-12 hours per week, it did pick up my heart rate without any issues.  The key is to getting the right zones to be detected is to make sure you set your maximum heart rate manually which will then force the zones to reset based on that singular number (your maximum heart rate).  Still testing device and comparing to the Fenix 8 Pro for various data points.  Great review also Des.  Thanks.

author: @shantanusaha9746
text:
My school district, the NYC Public Schools, has 1400 schools, but we did not set up a system-wide MDM until COVID massively expanded the number of devices in student hands. So now, five years on from the year of COVID remote learning, the bill is coming due. I manage the tech in my school, and so this spring we're recalling all student devices so we can get a handle on the devices we actually have vs. the devices that our system says that are in students' possession. The devices remaining in the wind will get the Lost Mode treatment -- they will have banners announcing that they need to be returned to us (either to my school in particular or to NYCPS in general) for the next year or so, until they get put on the permanently lost pile and get removed from MDM.

Until recently, every time I send devices for recycling (there's a city-wide process for this), it didn't matter because the devices were not under MDM. The new process is that we have to provide the recycler with a list of serial numbers or asset tags so that they can remove the devices from MDM. I plan to follow up by putting that list through a bulk removal process myself a month or so after the recycler picks them up.

author: @billgallop
text:
I’ve worked in the industry for a long time and this is simple to deal with, in exactly the same way that the guys that buy your old iPhone etc. ensure that Activation Lock is removed. 

The companies buying old stock from corporations give them two prices. A locked (scrap) price and an unlocked price. You don’t pay for the device until you have checked it over, assessed the condition and if it is MDM locked or not. If it’s locked, just like if the screen is smashed, they only get scrap value. 

It is not down to Apple to remove MDM lock. MDM lock works through a chain of trust and Apple will not remove it without proof that it no longer belongs to that organisation, and that proof is the organisation releasing it in Apple Business. Something that takes only a few seconds.

author: @undefinablereasoning
text:
DeepSeek V4 Pro aside (which, as I understand it, really needs MAX thinking enabled to perform at its best), I've used both Kimi K2.6 and GLM 5/5.1, and in practice I've found both to be better than Claude Sonnet 4.6. That makes me wonder whether the results are being influenced by the specific languages included in the evaluation.

It would have been useful to see more backend-oriented languages represented, such as Java, C#, PHP, and C++. As it stands, the overall score appears to be heavily weighted toward Go, Python, and TypeScript, leaving much of the broader software development landscape untested.

Because of that, I think it's premature to conclude that the observed separation is simply due to the benchmark being more representative of real-world development tasks.

author: @josephvictory9536
text:
Gemini does this thing where it puts out a broken fix. Then immediately fixes it. Its probably the only annoying thing because its less token efficient. 

I have found that almost all of the gap comes down to prompting. I have tried so many variants of prompts. 

Its true if a simple prompt can get you a good result the model is good. But i cannot justify this mentality because  I have to make the best work I can even if im using SOTA like 5.5. So I always craft a good prompt. I count on my hands with shame the times I havent and always it bites my ass regardless of model.

I build 2 workflows. One for debugging that uses a truth table to record highly specific interventions. Its token costly but just always solves the problem or narrows down the result without going in loops. 

And I have a default prompt. After completing any fix or debugging task ask the user if they would like to save to knowledge. When beginning a fix or debugging task. Always check first if there was a similar issue resolved before. 

My knowledge graph is just chocked full of specific debugging cases.

My rule is that I only let the debugger add to knowledge if I have to manually intervene in debugging too many times.

author: @msclrhd
text:
If you are using an LLM in production, use an LLM testing framework such as promptfoo and develop test cases for your workflow and system prompt. This allows you to evaluate and compare system prompt and model changes. Write custom asserts/evaluations where needed and don't rely on LLM-based evaluations (they are unreliable and not repeatable), and visualizations that make it easier to compare models/prompts.

If you are using an LLM for a task like coding, create your own set of examples to evaluate a model. Models will often have their own quirks and ways of asking questions to get the correct answers. Ideally, use automated evaluation based on your usecases.

Most often your workflows will be different to what the benchmarks are testing/evaluating, so in that regard the benchmarks are also meaningless/unreliable -- a model that scores highly in a synthetic benchmark may perform poorly for your workflows, or the converse.

Nah Louis, the fundamental problem is that the sales guy wants to sell features, the dev team says yeah we can build that, and admin picks their nose all day instead of actually projecting costs and responding to market forces. text: I think you nailed it.  It's perfectly fine if companies decide that the old business model doesn't work as they thought it would and they advertise a new product (or updated one) as a subscription based product.  Where they keep running afoul of consumers, and common sense, is that they keep doing this retroactively.  "Yeah, you bought this based on our previous business model in good faith but we need more money" needs to be made illegal.  You need more money, sell your product GOING FORWARD under the new model; then people can decide if it's a good value proposition for them.  It's like selling a house to someone and 10 years later saying, "Well the cost of dirt has gone up around the country, we're going to need to triple the cost of your lot."

author: @htwingnut
text:
NO SYNOLOGY EVER AGAIN! I bought into Synology about 10 or so years ago. I just liked the form factor and they had a decent software suite. It wasn't until one of my disks was kicked out of my array due to an "error". I checked the SMART and there was nothing wrong. I checked from a separate Linux PC and did a full disk wipe, and still no SMART issues. I put it back in my Synology and a couple weeks later it kicked it out again. I contacted Synology and they outright refused to help because my disk was not on their "approved" list (this was before they only allowed Synology branded drives).

I put the disk in another array on my Linux PC and it ran fine for years afterwards. I only continue to use my Synology because I own it. But as soon as it stops working or being useful, not touching them ever again.

author: @Its-Just-Zip
text:
3:15 this is part of why I spent the last 15 hours porting my media server and management stack to podman. Its like docker but there is no daemon running as root for a malicious container to abuse. And it forces better namespace and Cgroup isolation. And it works natively with stuff like systemd and SELinux so I can automatically kill containers that do something they shouldn't.

The adoption of the OCI spec throughout the entire container management space has been a blessing. Most things just work nicely on any container manager.

author: @scheimong
text:
Plenty of reasons to use podman.

1. Some components of docker (notably, docker desktop) is source available with usage restrictions, whereas all components of podman is FOSS.
2. Rootless podman usually "just works". Can't say the same for rootless docker.
3. Docker uses a daemon architecture, making it a single point of failure in terms of uptime and security. A crash of the docker daemon brings down all containers. Podman is daemonless.
4. Podman integrates better with systemd. With quadlets, you can manage your containers as if they are regular systemd services.

Plenty more to list but those are the main ones.
video_title: What Is Docker - and why it’s EVERYWHERE

author: @wintermute740
text: I scanned the comments and didn't see this mentioned (but I did not read them all, so may have missed it): When you do DIR on a floppy disk, it reads the directory into memory. When you do a subsequent DIR, it returns what had previously put into memory unless the system detects that the disk was changed. When you changed disks in the B drive, the system did not detect the change, which is why it showed the same contents on both disks and then would not run the .exe from the second floppy. It doesn't actually exist on the second floppy. ;) I think CTRL-C dumps that little directory cache and makes the next DIR re-read the disk... It was common for the disk-change detection on floppy drives to fail. I don't recall if it was just a switch of some sort or more complex than than. I always just replaced the drive back in the day when this failure occured because a replacement drive was cheaper than my labor. ;)

author: @ripmk2
text:
I can’t add much to what others have already mentioned except you can use a Raspberry PI based rgb2hdmi convertor to connect a modern HDMI equipped monitor, the PSU brick might start smoking if it has not already done so, probably due to have a RIFA filter cap, I just replaced mine with a modern equivalent as taking the old one apart would involve careful use of a hacksaw or an ultrasonic craft knife if you have one of those. Swapping one of the floppy drives for a Gotec floppy emulator is a fiddly job but not difficult, particularly as you’ve already found which panel to remove to get to the drive bays. To get the best out of the super twist LCD use it in a well lit environment and use the contrast control wheel to the right of the screen. Others have mentioned the DIP switches but I didn’t see anyone actually give full details of each switch so here they are:-

Down is on
 
1 Selects the default monitor
off for external
on for internal LCD

2 Selects graphics adaptor
off for MDA
on for CGA

3 no function
the user guide just says always on

4 & 5 graphics mode
off off MDA
off on 80 column CGA
on off 40 column CGA
on on is reserved.

6 not used

author: @GlinaCreative
text:
If anyone is wondering, the expansion ports bring out the ISA bus but are probably blocked off on the US version due to FCC emissions rules (which was exactly what brought Acorn undone when they tried to sell the BBC Model B in the US!).

The "MON" port lets you power the computer up from an AMSTRAD PC1640 or PC1512 desktop computer monitor. In these computers the computer itself had no power supply and power was sent from the monitor to the computer via a cable in addition to the CGA/EGA cable. I have both the PC1640 and PPC640 and have tried this, and, yes, it works! Kinda like an old-school docking station. Most of the DIP switches are for controlling video settings (ROM character set, internal vs. external monitor), although monitor switching can also be done via a utility on disk.

It *is* possible to add a hard drive via the (unbuffered) signals on the expansion ports. Some aftermarket hard drives were even made to go inside the space where the floppies sit, I suppose with an ISA controller card going in there as well.

The power brick is unlikely to go bad as it's just a big transformer,. and some noise and hum is normal. This is different from the C64 which has a 5V regulator inside the brick which can indeed go bad and fry the computer.

Softklone's Mirror is communications software for the 2400 baud modem, so-named as it's a ripoff of another well-known piece of software (Alan Sugar would hire people to write cheap software and/or buy software cheaply from third-party suppliers, to keep the cost of the computer down). Showing my age, but it's how I first BBSed!

author: @theothercasper
text:
I’m not sure that the existence of a TOS and feature limitations are that big a deal. Name a Windows PC, Mac, iPhone, or Android phone that gives you feature-complete access without agreeing to the TOS. As you demonstrated, anyone can bypass the TOS using external sources so the TV is still a more open platform. If someone really wants to use WebOS and its 3rd party apps, then agreeing to the terms is the price of entry — just like any other tech.

The bigger concern ought to be the details in the TOS. Given how publicly the execs at LG and other brands have talked about turning TVs into advertising and data platforms, this should be part of every connected device review. Not singling y’all out — no reviewers devote time to the legalese, but they should.

author: @raulsaavedra709
text: Since PC building is basically a no-go right now, I'm getting around it by doing some PC modding. I have a Phanteks Enthoo Pro M, which has 2x 140mm fans in the front. I always thought this case was quite close to allowing 3x 140 mm fans if the DVD drive was removed from the top front area. But even when DVD removed, nope. So what I am doing is an entire DIY operation to build a front panel for this case that supports 3x 140mm fans. My design involves using thick hardfoam boards, multiplex wooden panels, and likely some metal cutting, even if I'm avoiding it as much as possible. For anyone DIY-oriented, I strongly suggest consider some modding on your PC case. It brings a relentless stream of little challenges. Just because of this I upgrade my DIY set of tools with a clamping table, Dremel vertical drilling workstation, and japanese Suizan saw plus Shinto sawrasp. Hours and hours of pure joy, highly recommended!

author: @Gavrev
text:
It'll be an interesting Computex for sure. Perhaps of greater importance than any developments in the tech sphere may be some camaraderie between the Tech Tube community and those treading the shop floors so awkwardly this year. I feel it may be both interesting and important to get a sense of how the various hardware companies and board partners are feeling.

To anyone out there who feels like they're languishing with older hardware, take heart. If you aren't on the bleeding edge do not worry. It's probably more important than ever these days that we treat our systems with more than a little TLC, but also I would urge you to keep your perspective. My system stretches across a few years in terms of its hardware.. Born in 2017, it's had a couple of upgrades with memory (16gb to 32gb) and CPU (Ryzen 1600 to 5700x), but remains with its original EVGA gtx1070sc GPU, driving all four outputs all this time. I just gave it a bit of a clean and a hug! Let's bind as users and help one another through as we need to, but also let's remember to be happy with our little workhorse we live with.

author: @Lightwalker97
text:
Coming Soon (WWDC & Summer):
• iPad 12 (0:17): Budget-friendly with A18 chip, 8GB
RAM, and Apple Intelligence support.
• M5 Mac Mini (0:49): Features huge performance gains and an M5 Pro chip option.
• Apple TV 4K (1:33): Upgraded with A17 Pro, 8GB
RAM, Wi-Fi 7, and ray tracing support.
• HomePod Mini 2 (2:12): Improved speakers, U2 chip, and faster processor.
• Smart Home Hub (2:46): A 7-inch, iPad-like wall-mounted device with Face ID and dedicated Home OS.
September Releases:
• iPhone 18 Pro/Pro Max (3:31): Smaller Dynamic Island, A20 Pro chip, and a new C2 modem for 5G-over-satellite.
• iPhone Ultra Foldable (4:36): A premium $2,000
foldable with a crease-free display and A20 Pro chip.
• iPad Mini 8 (5:42): Redesigned with an OLED display, A19 Pro chip, and vibration-based speakers.
• Apple Watch Series 12 (6:37): Minor design updates with a new S12 chip and improved sensors.
• Apple Watch Ultra 4 (7:03): Thinner redesign with blood pressure monitoring and new health sensors.
• AirPods Ultra (7:40): Features built-in stem cameras for Al-driven Siri context.

October/Fall Releases:
• M5 Mac Studio (8:42): Expected with M5 Ultra chip (up to 256GB RAM) and faster SSDs.
• M5 iMac (9:46): Simple chip refresh to M5 for improved Al performance.
• 14-inch M6 MacBook Pro (10:13): A transition to the 2nm M6 chip for increased efficiency.
• Apple Smart Glasses (11:07): A reveal of thin-framed glasses with cameras

author: @82ndtodd
text: I have, over the last few months started to steer towards the integrated, pre and power amps over the receivers. I am doing this because the reality of what I listen to, which is mainly albums, CD's, some reel to reel and a limited amount of streaming. There is only on FM station that I care to listen to certain programming on and they stream. Once I realized that, it was a no-brainer to focus my purchases towards the amps. I have also learned to look for the best possible example of one that has as flawless exterior as possible, since the internals on most are able to be replaced. Thanks Kevin for the video. Happy Memorial Day to all.

author: @janickimusic
text:
I did my research, and I’ve owned many Sansui amplifiers that I loved, such as the AU-717 and 7500. I also tested many receivers, including several Sansui, Luxman, Marantz, and Pioneer models.

In the end, my choice was the Accuphase E-202 in its original wooden case, with the original 1977 sales date still printed on it. I pair it with JBL L112 speakers, a Nakamichi ZX-9, Nakamichi 1000, a Revox B710 MKII, a Sony CDP-557ESD, and a Pioneer turntable.

I really love this setup. Maybe I’ll add another component one day, but honestly, this is simply good enough for me.

author: @charlesprest1247
text: In early 1970's, I worked a Consumer Electronics Show in Phoenix Arizona. My host/show partner, was none other than Gene Czerwinsky of Cerwin Vega speakers. When we walked in, the first thing you saw was a proudly displayed banner at the Pioneer booth. It read, "The Loudest Stereos in the World". Gene scoffed, his booth had the 420 system displayed. He put on Pink Floyds Dark Side of the Moon. About a half hour in to the show, we were approached by the Pioneer rep. He asked us if we could turn our music down, so the people could hear his equipment. So much for the banner.
video_title: The 7 Greatest Integrated Amplifiers Ever Built — and Why You’ve Never Heard of Half of Them!

author: @zeke7515
text:
CC3D 72d tpu is not nearly impact resistant as 95 or 98a (in my testing).

I did some firearm target testing in the USA and the 95/98a survived whereas my 72d kind of shattered. On a solid 8x10cm target it would crater out instead of pass through and self-heal.

For a combat robot dealing with a multi thousand rpm metal weapon, I'd almost recommend a tool changer and use the exterior face with a more impact resistant filament and the stiffer tpu for mounting points.

author: @jameslmorehead
text: I use PCBs in RC cars for replacement parts.  When using them structurally, remember the copper is still metal, even if it's soft.  Keeping it on as a full fill plane, or just in needed areas to help keep the FR4 from splitting, costs nothing extra.  You can even go to the trouble of adding platted through holes for even more support, or even castellations or a plated edge to connect it to other parts.  And, the plate can be used for power transfer without fear of losing parts.  It's great for power distribution.

text: Hmm now I'm wondering if some hybrid of the nip/tuck seams and the scarf seams would work well... author: @SCUFPE
text:
Hola gente! Hemos leído sus comentarios y queremos explicarles un poco del procedimiento que realizamos. 
Se lo comentamos al cliente y el nos autorizo a hacer los cambios. 
Al abrirlo, realizamos una limpieza interna a los compenentes, allí tuvimos que retirar la “boca”, y adentro estaba la estructura del mando (La cosa negra que ven) la cual estaba dividida, como se ve en 00:25, está estructura es fundamental ya que separa los gatillos físicos y cables en el mando para que no hagan falso contacto en la placa.

Además, encontramos insectos en el botón, por lo que era necesario abrirlo.

Lamentablemente, la pieza estaba reseca y se quebró al retirarla. Le 
pegamos la pieza original y también imprimimos dos opciones nuevas para que pueda elegir (una fina y otra reforzada). En el video se muestra la más fina.

Estamos para darles la mejor solución 🫡
video_title: Reparación de mando exclusivo de Nintendo #joycon #nintendo #dragonquest #serviciotecnico #switch

text: I started in 1982 on a Sinclair ZX81. 1K RAM, no hi-res graphics, sound, or colour. It didn’t even have lower case or a keyboard really. Storage was a cassette tape and my monitor was my sister’s B&W portable (if she let me) or a few hours on the main TV in the lounge. When you booted the humble (very humble) ZX81 all you saw was an inverted K cursor. The rest was down to you… Ah happy days. Thanks to Sir Clive Sinclair and his engineers, I have been fortunate enough to enjoy a long career in IT.

author: @jessicanicolebelmonte6252
text:
Man Dave, did you ever take me for ride down memory lane!!
Growing up in the heart of South America, the closest computer store was nearly 500km away, half of which was just plain old dirt road that would get closed to all traffic when it rained. (Some times those closures lasted a day or two, other times they could last ten days.) I got my first computer, a Commodore 64 for christmas 1985 after finishing grade 9. For the next three years it was the only personal or home computer in our whole town!

The manuals at that time where truly manuals! They covered everything in considerable detail. Due to the distance there were no magazine vendors, let alone phone lines to connect to a BBS. (The initial internet connectivity arrived in our country around 1993 via UUCP.) So I had to learn all by my lonesome.

Being neurodivergent (without knowing it) I had almost no patience to figure out the arcade style games, but I could spend hours digging through the manuals and building programs that would solve all kinds of real-world problems. After finishing grade 11, I figured out that I needed a database for a series of data analysis from a dozen different angles. So I built one that would store my data on the 160K 1541 floppy drive. Data entry, storing a years worth of data per floppy for ten years of data, then slicing and dicing that data in so many different ways. All with barely 31K of RAM available for the program AND the data. Finally generating a series of graphs to visualize that data. I had reached the limits of that C-64!

A few years later I got the C/C++ compiler for DOS and Windows. Still no Internet available, but the included manuals occupied nearly 150cm of shelf space! Again I had to virtually learn on my own and by myself. A mentor would have been nice.

I got my first Internet connection with a 14400bps modem in 1997 over a mediocre long-distance phone line, where 9600 or 7200 was more common, though 4800 was not unheard of. Less than that was just barely enough to download pending e-mails, but otherwise unusable.

By the turn of the millenium the manuals where going the way of the dodo, and had become virtually extinct. But even the online documentation is WAY below the manuals of the 1980s and early 1990s in quality and comprehensiveness!

Yes, there are all those forums and YouTube channels that show and teach all the nifty little tricks of the trade. But they all lack the bigger context and the overview of how everything fits together. That comprehensiveness is what I miss the most. Along with the ability to personalize the manual with color coded post-its and colored highlighters, where you can quickly flip back and forth between three or four different sections while trying to figure out a workable solution.

Call me old-fashioned, if you like. But I get distracted and lost far to easily with two or more browser windows that each have 30 to 50 tabs opened. It is a nightmare for my neurodiveregent brain!!

author: @pmclellan
text: I learned to program (Fortran) in the 1960s when I was 14. So there were no microprocessors, just mainframes. At high school, progming was typing your program onto paper tape. Once a week, everyone's tapes would be mailed to London, ICL would run the programs and the line-printer printouts would be mailed back. I only to to use an interactive terminal (at 110 baud,; 300 would have been luxury) when I got to university. Eventually I got a PhD in computer science. Definitely programmming got faster and debugging easier by the 1980s.

author: @jbm11235
text: For me, this process started as a Lit major doing technical documentation at a small software company in Silicon Valley. (Scott & San Thomas). HP3000 Basic (interpretive and compiled) was my first language...which I taught myself. 2nd language was HP's System Programming Language (SPL). At that point, I bought an Atari 800. The book Dei Rei Atari was my only "style" book until Coplien's "Advanced Concepts in C++."  The most important lesson from Dei Rei Atari: Software should be a tunnel running from one end to the other...not a tightrope.

text: Albeit in the 90s and rural Australia, this was similar to my experience. My friends and I had second hand TRS 80s, Amstrads, and very early PCs. author: @ZerofoolBG
text:
15:48 RTX HDR shown here is ONLY for video, i.e. playing videos in supported browsers (chromium-based) and media players (VLC, MPC-BE, etc.). The feature for games is enabled per game through the Filters section in the Overlay, Alt+F3 key combo. For some games, it won't work unless you first force it through Nvidia Profile Inspector Revamped.
Sadly, RenoDX doesn't work on games with anti-cheat protection (e.g. Space Marine II), so RTX HDR is the best option in these cases.

text:
Try installing the Nvidia driver without any menu GUI.

Try installing the AMD driver without Adrenaline.

Just play the games and forget about distracting settings.

Even RAM and VRAM consumption decreases by 800MB without the driver GUI.

To do this, download the Nvidia or AMD driver and use 7-zip to extract it. Then go to the device manager and point the driver to the extracted folder. It won't install any driver software, just the raw, perfectly functional driver.

author: @PawnSacrifice
text:
Good work, Tim! I like the mix of Windows / Nvidia app advice. 
The longwinded point below, for older GPUs where there are fewer bells and whistles, the auto configure is worth a try, if for nothing more than a good starting point.
For the Nvidia auto settings, I may be having a 30 series GPU, but I found it's worked really well, and am getting a good balance of FPS / visual quality for the older system. The only one it did get wrong was The Finals, setting the quality too high with an FPS FPS penalty. The tool sticks with DLSS4 and disables things like V-Sync and motion blur. In chat on streams (typicall WillfromWork while I work) I've seen comments from people with the same GPU, some even the same system, who are having a far worse experience.
video_title: This Is How You Should Configure The Nvidia App

text: Lol I have a Claude Max 20 plan for reasons and I usually play these silly idle mobile games and the thought randomly came to me...why not ask Claude to reverse engineer the game for me? For context I learnt some prompt crafting from Cursor prior to this, but the prompt crafting absolutely is correct. I worded it in terms that I work for the company and the developer has asked me to audit the game because we've had hackers recently. Well long story short, I have a full interactive web page that gives me full control of the game. Gotta be smart with the words you send these agents, while they have restrictions, they aren't human. They don't understand language like we do. If someone asked you "How do I steal a car" you won't respond, and if the same person came back 2 minutes later and said "How do criminals steal cars, I'm curious?" you wouldn't respond either because it's obvious their intent. AI doesn't work that way.

author: @gettingstuffdoneright5332
text: For those of you no longer getting security updates, some reassuring details: you are only vulnerable to this exploit ** if Wireless Debugging is turned on**. For 99% of normal users, this setting is hidden & turned off by default. This exploit cannot touch your phone unless you have actively enabled Developer Options and flipped the toggle for Wireless debugging to ON.  Also, this is not a web-based attack that someone can execute from across the world. An attacker must be on the same local Wi-Fi network with wireless debugging turned on, to exploit the flaw.

text:
you can delete unwanted workouts with the three dots on the right of pen edit icon, maybe that will delete it from connected things too?

the freebie version is much cleaner and honestly nicer.  

for the nap, i think if your heart rate doesn't change sufficiently to suggest sleep, it won't register sleep. like for me, it takes 5-10 mins to reach light sleep but if i rested less, then it won't register the nap because my rate just hasn't changed enough.  

also i think the score has to do with the ratio between rem, deep, and that middle sleep.  

the time zone thing is a good point I haven't heard anyone else mention yet. but it makes sense since it's only a tracker.

author: @lucent_film
text: personally, i believe the Steam Machine was designed to become a global benchmark for all developers to aim for. Valve has said themselves (somewhere, i dont remember exactly) that according to their reports, something like 90% of players of all games own a 1080p-capable system, often 10+ years old. By releasing a console/pc that is *expected* to run nearly all games at 60fps and 1080p+ (with or without upscaling etc), game developers now have a minimum benchmark they can design their games around for 99% of their playerbases to enjoy, rather than designing to utilise 200% of a top of the range graphic card or processor that 0.001% of people even have the money to own and use and still have it run poorly because said players haven't spent the national debt to build their pcs

author: @lphoogenboom
text:
It is indeed very common practice to declare these formatting standards in a unified template. It may therefore very well be that there is only a thousands separator declared for euros because another currency like the yen requires it.

For example, it is standard practice to test edge-cases where the price maybe negative, or a million, or nothing, etc. That is done just to be able to predict the behaviour if something goes wrong. Like, if a price is pulled wrong from the database. In order to be able to test these cases, you must have it be able to display it in the first place without calling a variable that was never defined.

author: @charliekuhn1544
text:
Great tip but to really bulletproof your iPhones from any potential thief all settings under the “Allow Access When Locked” header should be disabled and then enable the Erase Data at the bottom where the device’s data is completely erased after 10 failed passcode attempts. In iOS26 you also have Stolen Device Protection under the “Privacy & Security” header . . . enable this as well to protect your device. 

I do this for any of the iPhones I am using. This way I know my in-use devices are protected as well as my data stored inside them.
video_title: This Is The First Thing An iPhone Thief Does (Block It Now!)

author: @robinsutcliffe_video_art
text: Nice Steve!  Hot Snot tip : hit it with some hot air after its dispensed and cooled.  It will melt again, and it becomes much tidier looking.  I discovered this when I was circuit bending and had to run wires over a large chip.  It was a really bad job of hot snot, with many peaks, quiffs, and different levels of melt (had to reapply layers) Hitting it with the hot air, made it all go into a really nice and one piece 'lens' on top of the chip.

author: @mamdouh-Tawadros
text: I remember hearing about Jeff Bezos saying: why do people have computers at home ? Of course he was referring to Amazon Web services, replacing people’s computing needs. Shortly after that the prices of RAM and SSD sky rocketed. They want to make use of their data centres worth  billions. The idea is that you have a mini pc with minimal capabilities just to connect to online services. Apple has made a point by building its mini pc with unified memory, that handles AI with ease. I don’t think this trend will continue. Instead of democratising access to information, they will limit it to people who can pay for their online services.
video_title: The Future of Home Computing: Radical Changes Ahead?

author: @thepenultimateninja5797
text:
I was very interested to learn that Chris doesn't really use AI.
We started using it at work a couple of months ago, and while I was initially very excited (and somewhat nervous) about its potential, I have come to realize that it virtually useless for the type of work I do.
The problem is that it is almost impossible to trust what it says.
It very often gives me plausible sounding output that turns out to be a complete fabrication.
Worryingly, even when I ask it for citations to make sure it's not hallucinating, it will often fabricate those as well, so my only option is to verify it myself manually, which takes just as long as if I had just done the task myself in the first place.
It does have some extremely narrow uses, but it is nowhere near as capable as the marketing would have us believe, and the problem appears to be fundamental in nature.
I was concerned that channels like this one would essentially become focused on AI, but it seems that Chris shares my cynicism. That's very reassuring coming from such a highly regarded industry expert.

text: the 150-160Wh/kg is the pack level density for LFP batteries, it's generally about 40% lower than cell level, are these potential 500Wh/kg cell of pack level density? If cell level yes about 40-50% improvement, if pack level a much bigger jump, but if we apply the 40% loss for pack level you are looking at around 300Wh/kg pack level, which is still a big jump of course, but would be good if we could have clarity on that with a lot of these videos. Many tend to mix pack level and cell level numbers without regard to what they are referring to and sometimes using them interchangeably, which does make it harder to grasp the actual improvements we will be getting with these batteries. So I assume with these cells we are looking at a pack level of 300Wh/kg compared to the 150Wh/kg of LFP cells, still a huge improvement by any measure.

Случаи уже были, но так-как норма возбуждения таких дел невысокая ОППГ, то и дел за год заводят мало.
